comment_text.sn17.23:1.4: Also at AN 2.132:1.3 and  AN 4.176:3.2. | An entire Saṁyutta is devoted to Citta (SN 41), the foremost layman in teaching Dhamma (AN 1.250). | Hatthaka, the foremost in attracting a congregation (AN 1.251), is featured especially in AN 7.23 and AN 7.24.

root_text.sn17.23:1.3: saddhā, bhikkhave, upāsikā ekaputtakaṁ piyaṁ manāpaṁ evaṁ sammā āyācamānā āyāceyya:
root_text.sn17.23:1.4: ‘tādiso, tāta, bhavāhi yādiso citto ca gahapati hatthako ca āḷavako’ti.
root_text.sn17.23:1.5: Esā, bhikkhave, tulā etaṁ pamāṇaṁ mama sāvakānaṁ upāsakānaṁ, yadidaṁ citto ca gahapati hatthako ca āḷavako.
root_text.sn17.23:1.6: Sace kho tvaṁ, tāta, agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajasi;
root_text.sn17.23:1.8: Esā, bhikkhave, tulā etaṁ pamāṇaṁ mama sāvakānaṁ bhikkhūnaṁ, yadidaṁ sāriputtamoggalānā.
root_text.sn17.23:1.9: Mā ca kho tvaṁ, tāta, sekhaṁ appattamānasaṁ lābhasakkārasiloko anupāpuṇātūti.
root_text.sn17.23:1.10: Tañce, bhikkhave, bhikkhuṁ sekhaṁ appattamānasaṁ lābhasakkārasiloko anupāpuṇāti, so tassa hoti antarāyāya.

translation_text.sn17.23:1.3: A faithful laywoman with a dear and beloved only son would rightly appeal to him,
translation_text.sn17.23:1.4: ‘My darling, please be like the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī.’
translation_text.sn17.23:1.5: These are a standard and a measure for my male lay disciples, that is, the householder Citta and Hatthaka of Āḷavī.
translation_text.sn17.23:1.6: ‘But my darling, if you go forth from the lay life to homelessness,
translation_text.sn17.23:1.7: please be like Sāriputta and Moggallāna.’
translation_text.sn17.23:1.8: These are a standard and a measure for my monk disciples, that is, Sāriputta and Moggallāna.
translation_text.sn17.23:1.9: ‘And my darling, may you not come into possessions, honor, and popularity while you’re still a trainee and haven’t achieved your heart’s desire.’
translation_text.sn17.23:1.10: If a trainee who hasn’t achieved their heart’s desire comes into possessions, honor, and popularity it’s an obstacle for them.
translation_text.sn17.23:1.11: So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …”
